topics.title: Inheritance in Java
topics.description: Inheritance is an OOP mechanism where a child class (subclass) acquires the fields and methods of a parent class (superclass) using the `extends` keyword. Java supports single-class inheritance only — one direct parent per class — but a child can override parent methods, add new fields/methods, and use `super` to call the parent's implementation. The root of every class hierarchy is `java.lang.Object`.
topics.story: Think of biological inheritance. A child inherits traits from parents — same eye color, similar height — but may also have unique traits and override some inherited ones. In code: Vehicle defines `fuelUp()` and `move()`. Car extends Vehicle — gets those for free, adds `openTrunk()`, and overrides `move()` with smoother acceleration. ElectricCar extends Car — overrides `fuelUp()` to 'charge instead'. You only write what's new or different.
topics.analogy: Inheritance = a job template at a company. Every Employee has `name`, `salary`, `clockIn()`. A Manager is an Employee but additionally has `teamSize` and `approveLeave()`. A CEO is a Manager with `setBudget()`. Each level inherits all the work from every level above — a CEO can still clockIn() — and adds or specialises what's needed. No code duplication across levels.
topics.memoryAnchor: Child IS-A Parent (Car IS-A Vehicle). `extends` keyword = inheritance. `super` = access parent. Method table (vtable) — each class has a table of method pointers; child's table copies parent's and replaces overridden ones. Mnemonic: 'PIER' — Parent Inherited, Extended, Replaced.
topics.firstPrinciples: Without inheritance: copy-paste all Vehicle code into Car, Truck, Bus — four near-identical classes, four maintenance nightmares. With inheritance: common code lives once in Vehicle; subclasses add or override only their differences. This is the DRY principle at the class level. The JVM dispatch mechanism (dynamic dispatch / vtable lookup) enables the same code to work on any subclass at runtime.
topics.bruteForce: Without inheritance: Animal, Dog, Cat, Bird all define name, age, eat(), sleep() separately. Four copies of identical code. When you fix a bug in eat(), you fix it four times. Without polymorphism, you need if-instanceof chains everywhere.
topics.optimizedApproach: With inheritance: Animal defines the shared state and behaviour. Dog, Cat, Bird extend Animal — each only writes what differs (sound(), movement()). Code that works on Animal works on all subclasses automatically. Add a new animal type: create the class, extend Animal, done — no existing code changes.
topics.whenToUse: Use inheritance when: there is a true IS-A relationship (Car IS-A Vehicle, not just similar to), you want to share common implementation (not just an interface contract), and the Liskov Substitution Principle holds — anywhere you use a Vehicle, a Car must work correctly. Prefer composition over inheritance when you need flexibility at runtime or when the IS-A relationship is forced.

examples.title: Basic Inheritance — extends and super
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":52,"lineCode":"Dog d = new Dog(\"Rex\", 3, \"Labrador\");","variables":{"d":"ref@0x100"},"phase":"CREATE","annotation":"new Dog: first calls super(name, age) which runs Animal constructor. Then Dog sets breed. One heap object with ALL fields: name, age, breed"},{"line":53,"lineCode":"d.eat();","variables":{"d.name":"Rex"},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Dog has no eat() — JVM looks up vtable, finds Animal.eat(). Calls it with this=d. 'name' resolves to d.name='Rex'"},{"line":55,"lineCode":"d.bark();","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Dog has bark() in its own vtable entry. Calls directly"},{"line":60,"lineCode":"GuideDog g = new GuideDog(\"Buddy\", 5, \"Golden\", \"John\");","variables":{"g":"ref@0x200"},"phase":"CREATE","annotation":"Chain: GuideDog super → Dog super → Animal constructor. g has ALL fields: name, age, breed, owner"},{"line":62,"lineCode":"g.eat();","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"GuideDog → Dog → Animal.eat() found. Three-level vtable lookup"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph BT
    GuideDog -->|extends| Dog
    Dog -->|extends| Animal
    Animal -->|extends implicit| Object
    
    subgraph GuideDog_heap_object
      F1[name, age — from Animal]
      F2[breed — from Dog]
      F3[owner — from GuideDog]
    end
examples.pseudocode:
INHERITANCE CHAIN:
  GuideDog extends Dog extends Animal extends Object

OBJECT CREATION new GuideDog(...):
  1. super(name, age, breed) → Dog constructor
  2. super(name, age)        → Animal constructor
  3. Dog sets breed
  4. GuideDog sets owner
  → ONE heap object with ALL fields from all levels

METHOD LOOKUP (vtable):
  call g.eat() → GuideDog vtable → Dog vtable → Animal.eat() ✓
examples.explanation: extends creates an IS-A chain. A Dog object on the heap contains ALL fields from Animal (name, age) plus its own (breed) — one unified object. The constructor chain MUST start with super(...) as the first line — otherwise the parent fields are never initialized. Method dispatch walks the vtable up the chain until the method is found.
examples.realWorldUse: Spring framework: DispatcherServlet extends FrameworkServlet extends HttpServletBean extends HttpServlet. Each level adds Spring-specific functionality while inheriting the servlet lifecycle from the standard API. Jackson's JsonDeserializer<T> is extended by application-specific deserializers to inherit the framework wiring while customising only parse logic.

examples.title: Method Overriding — @Override and super()
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":27,"lineCode":"@Override double area() { return Math.PI * radius * radius; }","variables":{},"phase":"DEFINE","annotation":"@Override verifies at compile time that Shape has an area() method to override. Without @Override, a typo like arae() would compile silently as a new method"},{"line":31,"lineCode":"return super.describe() + \" (circle r=\" + radius + \")\"","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"super.describe() calls Shape.describe(). Inside Shape.describe(), area() is called — but 'this' is the Circle instance! So Circle.area() executes. This is runtime polymorphism"},{"line":57,"lineCode":"System.out.println(c.describe());","variables":{"c":"Circle@0x100"},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Circle.describe() → calls super.describe() which calls this.area() → Circle.area() (not Shape.area()). Result: 78.54"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A[c.describe() called] --> B[Circle.describe() runs]
    B -->|super.describe()| C[Shape.describe() runs]
    C -->|calls this.area()| D{Which area?}
    D -->|'this' is Circle!| E[Circle.area() → 78.54]
    E --> F[Shape.describe returns 'red shape, area=78.54']
    F --> G[Circle.describe appends ' (circle r=5.0)']
examples.pseudocode:
@Override:
  compile-time check: parent must have this method
  runtime: child's version called through vtable

super.method():
  explicitly call parent's version
  useful for extending (not replacing) parent behaviour

RUNTIME POLYMORPHISM:
  Shape s = new Circle(...);
  s.area() → Circle.area() (not Shape.area!)
  JVM decides at runtime based on actual object type
examples.explanation: @Override is a compile-time annotation — prevents typos and missing matches. super.describe() inside a Circle's override calls the parent's version, enabling extension rather than complete replacement. Critically: when Shape.describe() internally calls area(), Java uses runtime polymorphism — it calls the most-derived override (Circle.area()), not Shape.area(). This is dynamic dispatch.
examples.realWorldUse: Spring's @RequestMapping uses method overriding: base DispatcherServlet has a doDispatch() that calls processHandlerException() — when you @Override processHandlerException in a subclass, Spring automatically calls YOUR version. Template Method Pattern in JUnit: TestCase.setUp(), tearDown() — override these to plug in test-specific behaviour.

examples.title: super Keyword — Accessing Parent
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":25,"lineCode":"super(make, year);","variables":{"make":"Tesla","year":2023},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"super() in constructor: invokes Vehicle(make, year). Sets this.make and this.year. MUST be first statement"},{"line":31,"lineCode":"return super.status() + \" | battery=\" + batteryLevel + \"%\";","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"super.status() explicitly calls Vehicle.status() — gets the base string. Then appends battery info. Extends, not replaces"},{"line":36,"lineCode":"void refuel(double amount) { batteryLevel = ...","variables":{},"phase":"DEFINE","annotation":"Completely replaces Vehicle.refuel(). No super.refuel() call — intentional. An electric car doesn't pump fuel"},{"line":42,"lineCode":"refuel(50);","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"Inside ElectricCar method: refuel(50) calls THIS class's refuel() — not Vehicle's. Dynamic dispatch applies even within the same object"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A[super in constructor] -->|first line only| B[Parent constructor runs]
    C[super.method()] -->|any method| D[Parent's version of that method]
    E[super.field] -->|access parent field if shadowed| F[Parent's field]
    G[NO super keyword] --> H[Child's own vtable lookup applies]
examples.pseudocode:
super() in constructor:
  → calls parent constructor
  → MUST be first statement
  → JVM auto-inserts super() if not present

super.method():
  → skip vtable lookup, directly call parent's version
  → can be in any method, any line

super.field:
  → access parent field if shadowed by child field of same name
examples.explanation: super has two forms: (1) super() in a constructor — calls the parent constructor, must be the very first statement, JVM inserts a no-arg super() automatically if omitted (fails if parent has no no-arg constructor). (2) super.method() / super.field — explicitly accesses the parent's version in any method, bypassing the vtable for that one call. Used to extend (not fully replace) parent behaviour.
examples.realWorldUse: Spring Security WebSecurityConfigurerAdapter (pre-Spring 5.7): override configure(HttpSecurity http) and call super.configure(http) to keep the default security rules before adding your own. JUnit 4 test classes: super.setUp() to run the parent fixture setup. Android Activity lifecycle: always call super.onCreate(), super.onDestroy() — the framework depends on it for memory management.

examples.title: Object Class — The Root of All
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":30,"lineCode":"System.out.println(p1.toString());","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"No toString() override — JVM uses Object.toString() = getClass().getName() + '@' + Integer.toHexString(hashCode())"},{"line":31,"lineCode":"System.out.println(p1.equals(p2));","variables":{"p1":"ref@0x100","p2":"ref@0x200"},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"No equals() override — Object.equals() uses ==. 0x100 != 0x200 → false even though values are same"},{"line":34,"lineCode":"Object o = p1;","variables":{"o":"ref@0x100"},"phase":"ASSIGN","annotation":"Widening reference conversion: Point IS-A Object. 'o' holds the same reference as p1 — no new object created"},{"line":35,"lineCode":"o instanceof Point","variables":{},"phase":"CHECK","annotation":"instanceof checks the actual runtime type of the heap object. Object at 0x100 is a Point → true"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph BT
    Point -->|implicit extends| Object
    String -->|extends| Object
    Integer -->|extends| Object
    
    subgraph Object_methods
      T[toString()]
      E[equals(Object)]
      H[hashCode()]
      G[getClass()]
      C[clone()]
      W[wait / notify]
    end
examples.pseudocode:
Every class implicitly:
  extends Object (if no explicit parent)

Object provides defaults:
  toString()  → "ClassName@hexHash"
  equals(o)   → this == o (reference)
  hashCode()  → identity hash (address-based)
  getClass()  → runtime Class object
  clone()     → shallow copy (Cloneable)

BEST PRACTICE: override toString, equals, hashCode
examples.explanation: Every Java class implicitly extends Object. Object provides universal methods: toString() for display (override for readability), equals() for logical equality (override with hashCode), hashCode() for collections, getClass() for runtime type. Widening: any object can be stored in an Object reference — no cast needed. Narrowing: Object back to a specific type requires a cast and may throw ClassCastException.
examples.realWorldUse: Java generics erase to Object at runtime — List<String> becomes List<Object> in bytecode. Collections.sort() works on any Comparable because Object is the root. Spring's BeanFactory stores beans as Object. Logging frameworks call obj.toString() — always override it for meaningful log output. JUnit's assertEquals(expected, actual) calls expected.equals(actual) — override equals() for correct test assertions.

examples.title: Inheritance Chain — Constructor and Method Dispatch
examples.tracerSteps: [{"line":24,"lineCode":"B b = new B();","variables":{},"phase":"CREATE","annotation":"new B(): JVM first calls A() constructor"},{"line":4,"lineCode":"A() { System.out.println(\"A constructor\"); show(); }","variables":{},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"A constructor runs. It calls show() — but 'this' is a B object! Dynamic dispatch → B.show() is called"},{"line":12,"lineCode":"void show() { System.out.println(\"B.show() value=\" + value); }","variables":{"value":0},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"DANGER: B.value is declared as 42 but field initializers run AFTER super() returns. At this point value=0 (default)!"},{"line":9,"lineCode":"B() { super(); System.out.println(\"B constructor\"); }","variables":{"value":42},"phase":"CONT","annotation":"Now B constructor continues. Field value=42 is set. But show() was already called with value=0"},{"line":38,"lineCode":"A ref = c; ref.show();","variables":{"ref":"C@0x300"},"phase":"CALL","annotation":"ref is type A at compile time, but runtime object is C. JVM dispatches to C.show() — polymorphism"}]
examples.flowchartMermaid:
graph TD
    A[new B()] --> B1[JVM calls super() = A()]
    B1 --> B2[A constructor body runs]
    B2 -->|calls show()| B3[POLYMORPHIC: B.show() called!]
    B3 -->|B.value not yet initialized| B4[value = 0]
    B4 --> B5[A constructor finishes]
    B5 --> B6[B field initializers run: value=42]
    B6 --> B7[B constructor body finishes]
examples.pseudocode:
CONSTRUCTOR ORDER (new C()):
  1. C() → super() → B() → super() → A()
  2. A constructor body executes (A fields initialized)
  3. B field initializers run (value=42 set)
  4. B constructor body executes
  5. C field initializers run
  6. C constructor body executes

DANGER: A() calls show() → polymorphic dispatch → C.show()
  but C/B fields not yet initialized at that point!
examples.explanation: Constructor chain runs from top (root) down. Field initializers run AFTER the super() call returns. The deadly pitfall: calling an overridable method from a constructor. A() calls show() — but this is a B instance, so B.show() is called before B's field initializers have run — value is 0, not 42. Rule: never call overridable (non-final, non-private) methods in constructors. Use final or private methods in constructors instead.
examples.realWorldUse: This is item 19 in Effective Java: 'Design and document for inheritance or else prohibit it.' Spring's AbstractApplicationContext constructor calls onRefresh() which is overridden by subclasses — the framework documents this carefully. Android View constructors call init() — a common source of NPE bugs when developers override init() and access fields not yet set.

problems.title: Extend a Shape Class
problems.description: Given a `Shape` class with `String color` and `area()` returning 0.0, create a `Circle(double radius, String color)` and `Square(double side, String color)` that extend it. Override `area()` in each. Create one of each and print their areas.
problems.hint1: Use `super(color)` in each constructor to pass color to Shape.
problems.hint2: Circle: `return Math.PI * radius * radius;` Square: `return side * side;`
problems.hint3: Add `@Override` before `area()` in each subclass.
problems.editorial: super(color) forwards the color argument to Shape's constructor. @Override ensures the method signatures match. Shape.toString() calls area() — dynamic dispatch routes to the subclass override, so the printed area is correct for each concrete type.

problems.title: Predict Constructor Order
problems.hint1: C() implicitly calls super() → B constructor first.
problems.hint2: B() implicitly calls super() → A constructor first.
problems.hint3: Print order: A runs first, B second, C third — deepest ancestor first.
problems.editorial: Constructors run from the TOP of the hierarchy down. super() is implicit when not written — JVM inserts it. A() runs first, then B() resumes after super() returns, then C() resumes. The deepest ancestor always initialises first.

problems.title: super.method() — Extend Don't Replace
problems.description: Create `Logger` with method `log(String msg)` that prints `[LOG] msg`. Extend it as `TimestampLogger` which overrides `log()` to prepend a timestamp and then calls `super.log(msg)` so the `[LOG]` prefix is still applied.
problems.hint1: TimestampLogger.log(): prepend timestamp string, then call super.log(timestampedMsg).
problems.hint2: Use System.currentTimeMillis() or a static counter for the timestamp.
problems.hint3: Output should be: `[LOG] [ts=12345] hello` — super adds [LOG], child adds timestamp.
problems.constraints: Use super.log() — do not duplicate the [LOG] prefix logic.
problems.editorial: super.log(msg) explicitly calls Logger.log() — bypassing the vtable for that one call. This is the extension pattern: add behaviour before/after the parent's implementation rather than replacing it. Used in servlet filters, Spring interceptors, AOP before/after advice.

problems.title: IS-A Check With instanceof
problems.description: Create a three-level hierarchy: `Animal → Dog → GoldenRetriever`. Using `instanceof`, check which classes each object IS-A. Implement `checkInstanceOf(Object obj)` that returns a `String` describing which types the object is an instance of.
problems.hint1: A GoldenRetriever IS-A Dog IS-A Animal IS-A Object — instanceof returns true for all.
problems.hint2: Check in order: GoldenRetriever, Dog, Animal, Object — order doesn't affect result but most specific first is clearest.
problems.hint3: Build: `if (obj instanceof GoldenRetriever) result += "GoldenRetriever ";`
problems.editorial: instanceof tests the ACTUAL runtime type. A GoldenRetriever IS-A Dog, IS-A Animal, IS-A Object — all true. A Dog is NOT a GoldenRetriever — false. instanceof enables safe downcasting. Java 16+ pattern matching: `if (obj instanceof Dog d) { d.bark(); }` — no separate cast needed.

problems.title: toString() Chain With super
problems.description: Create hierarchy `Vehicle → Car → SportsCar` where each class has unique fields and each `toString()` calls `super.toString()` to build a cumulative string. Verify the final string includes all three levels' data.
problems.hint1: Vehicle.toString(): `return "Vehicle[make=" + make + "]"`
problems.hint2: Car.toString(): `return super.toString() + " Car[doors=" + doors + "]"`
problems.hint3: SportsCar.toString(): `return super.toString() + " Sports[turbo=" + turbo + "]"`
problems.editorial: super.toString() in each level calls the parent's version first, then appends the current level's data. The final string aggregates data from all three levels. This demonstrates the 'extension' use of super.method() — each level builds on what came before rather than discarding it.

problems.title: Polymorphic Array of Shapes
problems.description: Create `Shape → Circle(radius) → Triangle(base, height)`. Store mixed instances in a `Shape[]`. Implement `totalArea(Shape[] shapes)` that sums all areas using polymorphism — no instanceof checks needed.
problems.hint1: Shape[] shapes = { new Circle(3), new Triangle(4,5), new Circle(7) };
problems.hint2: totalArea: `double sum = 0; for (Shape s : shapes) sum += s.area(); return sum;`
problems.hint3: Each call to s.area() dispatches to the actual subclass override at runtime.
problems.editorial: s.area() is a polymorphic call — the JVM dispatches to Circle.area() or Triangle.area() based on the actual object type, not the reference type Shape. No instanceof or casting needed. This is the power of polymorphism: totalArea works correctly for any future Shape subclass without modification.

problems.title: Employee Payroll Hierarchy
problems.description: Build `Employee(name, baseSalary)` with `calculatePay()` returning baseSalary. Extend it as `Manager(name, baseSalary, bonus)` where calculatePay() adds bonus, and `Contractor(name, hourlyRate, hoursWorked)` where calculatePay() = hourlyRate × hoursWorked. Print payroll for a mixed list.
problems.hint1: Manager.calculatePay(): `return super.calculatePay() + bonus;`
problems.hint2: Contractor doesn't call super — it has a completely different pay formula.
problems.hint3: Store all in `List<Employee>`, iterate and call calculatePay() polymorphically.
problems.editorial: Manager.calculatePay() uses super.calculatePay() to get base salary then adds bonus — extending, not replacing. Contractor completely overrides the formula — passes 0 to super just to satisfy the constructor. The for-each loop calls calculatePay() polymorphically — correct result for each type without any instanceof.

problems.title: Abstract Payment Processor
problems.description: Create abstract `PaymentProcessor` with abstract method `processPayment(double amount)` and a concrete `logTransaction(double amount)` that prints `Processing $X.XX`. Extend as `CreditCardProcessor` and `PayPalProcessor`, each with its own processPayment(). Call logTransaction from each.
problems.hint1: Abstract class: `abstract class PaymentProcessor { abstract void processPayment(double amount); }`
problems.hint2: Each subclass calls `super.logTransaction(amount)` inside processPayment() to log.
problems.hint3: Or: implement a template method in the abstract class that calls logTransaction then processPayment.
problems.editorial: Abstract classes can have both concrete (logTransaction) and abstract (processPayment) methods. Subclasses must implement all abstract methods. logTransaction provides shared behaviour available to all processors — a common pattern called the Template Method, where the base class defines the algorithm skeleton and subclasses fill in the steps.

problems.title: Override hashCode and equals in Subclass
problems.description: Create `Person(String name, int age)` with proper equals/hashCode. Extend it as `Employee(name, age, employeeId)` — override equals/hashCode to also compare employeeId. Show that two Employees with same name/age but different IDs are NOT equal, but two with same name/age/id ARE.
problems.hint1: Employee.equals(): call super.equals(o) first, then also compare employeeId.
problems.hint2: Employee.hashCode(): return Objects.hash(super.hashCode(), employeeId).
problems.hint3: Be careful: super.equals() checks instanceof Person — a Person and Employee with same name/age won't be equal (different types).
problems.editorial: Extending equals in subclasses: call super.equals() first to verify parent fields match, then check the extra subclass field. Include employeeId in hashCode via Objects.hash(super.hashCode(), employeeId). Note: the symmetry problem — a Person.equals(Employee) may return true while Employee.equals(Person) returns false. Effective Java recommends using getClass() instead of instanceof for this reason in non-sealed hierarchies.

problems.title: Downcast and ClassCastException
problems.description: Given a `Shape[]` containing Circles and Triangles stored as Shapes, safely extract all Circles using instanceof before downcasting. Also demonstrate what happens when you downcast without checking (ClassCastException). Return the count of Circle instances found.
problems.hint1: Upcast (widening): `Shape s = new Circle(5);` — always safe, implicit.
problems.hint2: Downcast (narrowing): `Circle c = (Circle) s;` — only safe if s IS-A Circle.
problems.hint3: Safe pattern: `if (s instanceof Circle c) { /* use c */ }` (Java 16+ pattern matching).

testCases.output: CCE: class Q10$Triangle cannot be cast to class Q10$Circle

problems.editorial: Upcast (Shape s = new Circle()) is always safe — no check needed. Downcast (Circle c = (Circle) s) requires the object to actually be a Circle at runtime — otherwise ClassCastException. Always use instanceof before downcasting in production code. Java 16+ pattern matching `if (s instanceof Circle c)` combines the check and cast in one step.

problems.title: Template Method Pattern
problems.description: Implement a `DataProcessor` abstract class with a `process()` template method that calls: `readData()` → `transformData()` → `writeData()`. Subclasses override the three steps. Implement `CsvProcessor` and `JsonProcessor` with different read/transform/write implementations.
problems.hint1: process() is final and concrete in DataProcessor: calls readData(), transformData(), writeData() in order.
problems.hint2: readData(), transformData(), writeData() are abstract — subclasses implement them.
problems.hint3: Mark process() as final to prevent subclasses from changing the algorithm order.

testCases.output:
Reading CSV
Parsing CSV rows
Writing CSV output

Reading JSON
Parsing JSON tree
Writing JSON output

problems.editorial: Template Method pattern: the parent defines the algorithm (process() — fixed order of steps) and delegates the variable parts to abstract methods. Subclasses customise the steps without changing the order. Used in JUnit (setUp → test → tearDown), HttpServlet (service → doGet/doPost), Spring Batch (open → read → process → write → close).

problems.title: Liskov Substitution — Verify IS-A
problems.description: Create a `Bird` class with `fly()` printing 'flying'. Extend as `Eagle` (can fly) and `Penguin` (CANNOT fly). Show that Penguin extending Bird and inheriting fly() violates LSP (Liskov Substitution Principle). Fix by redesigning: `Bird → CanFlyBird → Eagle` and `Bird → Penguin`.
problems.hint1: LSP: if S extends T, any code that works on T must work on S without surprising behaviour.
problems.hint2: Penguin.fly() throwing UnsupportedOperationException violates LSP — code expecting a Bird to fly will break.
problems.hint3: Fix: don't put fly() in Bird. Create FlyingBird extends Bird with fly(). Eagle extends FlyingBird. Penguin extends Bird.
problems.editorial: LSP: subclasses must be substitutable for their superclass. If Penguin extends Bird but can't fly, any code using Bird breaks for Penguin. The fix: don't put capabilities in the base class that not all subclasses have. Move fly() to a FlyingBird subtype. The compiler then prevents passing Penguins where FlyingBirds are expected — fail fast at compile time.

problems.title: Covariant Return Types
problems.description: Java allows a subclass to narrow (covary) the return type of an overridden method. Create `Animal` with `Animal create()` returning `new Animal()`. Extend as `Dog` with `@Override Dog create()` — the return type is narrowed from Animal to Dog. Show this compiles and that the result is a Dog.
problems.hint1: Dog.create() return type is Dog (a subtype of Animal) — this is valid covariant narrowing.
problems.hint2: Animal ref = new Dog(); ref.create() calls Dog.create() and returns a Dog at runtime.
problems.hint3: Use getClass().getSimpleName() to verify the returned type at runtime.
problems.editorial: Covariant return types (Java 5+): an overriding method may declare a return type that is a subtype of the parent's return type. Dog.create() returns Dog instead of Animal — still @Override valid. Caller using a Dog reference gets a Dog directly (no cast). Polymorphic call through Animal reference still dispatches to Dog.create() — returning a Dog at runtime.

problems.title: Sealed Hierarchy (Java 17+)
problems.description: Create a sealed class `Shape` that permits only `Circle`, `Rectangle`, `Triangle` as subclasses. Use pattern matching switch to compute area without instanceof chains. Demonstrate that adding a new subclass outside the permitted list causes a compile error.
problems.hint1: `sealed class Shape permits Circle, Rectangle, Triangle {}`
problems.hint2: Subclasses must be `final` or themselves `sealed` or `non-sealed`.
problems.hint3: Pattern switch: `switch (shape) { case Circle c -> Math.PI*c.r*c.r; case Rectangle r -> r.w*r.h; ... }`
problems.editorial: Sealed classes restrict which classes can extend/implement them — the compiler knows the complete set. Pattern matching switch becomes exhaustive — no default needed because all permitted types are handled. Adding a case for a 4th shape (Hexagon) would require modifying the permits clause. Used in Java's own java.lang.constant.ConstantDesc and in Domain-Driven Design algebraic data types.

problems.title: Mixins via Multiple Interface Inheritance
problems.description: Java classes can implement multiple interfaces. Create interfaces `Flyable` (with default `fly()`) and `Swimmable` (with default `swim()`). Create `Duck` that implements both. Also create `FlyingFish` that implements both. Demonstrate method resolution when both interfaces define `describe()`.
problems.hint1: Interfaces with default methods: `interface Flyable { default void fly() { ... } }`
problems.hint2: Duck implements Flyable, Swimmable — inherits both default methods.
problems.hint3: If both interfaces have `default describe()`, Duck MUST override it — compile error otherwise.

testCases.output: I can fly and I can swim

problems.editorial: When two interfaces both provide a default method with the same signature, the implementing class MUST override it — otherwise a compile error: 'inherits unrelated defaults'. The class can delegate to a specific interface version using `Flyable.super.describe()` syntax. This is Java's solution to the diamond problem — explicit override required, no silent ambiguity.

problems.title: Fragile Base Class — Private vs Protected
problems.description: Demonstrate the fragile base class problem: a superclass changes its internals, breaking a subclass that relied on those internals. Create `Counter` with `increment()` and `incrementBy(n)`. Subclass `CountingList` overrides `incrementBy()` calling super `n` times. Show that if `Counter.increment()` calls `incrementBy(1)` internally, the subclass double-counts.
problems.hint1: If Counter.increment() calls this.incrementBy(1) — and CountingList overrides incrementBy() — then calling increment() on a CountingList calls CountingList.incrementBy(1) which calls super n times... leading to over-counting.
problems.hint2: Fix: use private helper methods in Counter instead of self-calls through overridable methods.
problems.hint3: Demonstrate the double-count, then show the fix.
problems.editorial: Fragile base class: when Counter.increment() calls incrementBy(1) via dynamic dispatch, the subclass's incrementBy() gets called — causing logCount to track increment() calls too. Fix: increment() calls a private addToCount(1) — private methods are NOT overridable, so LoggingCounter.incrementBy() is bypassed. Effective Java Item 19: document self-use of overridable methods, or use private helpers to avoid this.

problems.title: Visitor Pattern Over Inheritance
problems.description: Implement the Visitor pattern: separate an operation (computing tax) from the object hierarchy (Product types). Create `Visitor` interface with `visit(Food)`, `visit(Electronics)`. `TaxCalculator` implements Visitor. Each Product subclass has `accept(Visitor v)` that calls `v.visit(this)`.
problems.hint1: interface Visitor { double visit(Food f); double visit(Electronics e); }
problems.hint2: Product.accept(Visitor v) is abstract. Food.accept calls v.visit(this) — 'this' is Food.
problems.hint3: TaxCalculator: Food tax = 5%, Electronics tax = 18%. Return amount * taxRate.
problems.editorial: Visitor decouples operations from the class hierarchy. Adding a new operation (DiscountCalculator) requires only a new Visitor class — no changes to Product hierarchy. The double-dispatch trick: p.accept(visitor) calls visitor.visit(this) — 'this' is the concrete type (Food or Electronics), so the correct overloaded visit() is called at runtime. Used in Java compiler AST visitors, Lombok's @VisitorPattern, Apache POI.

problems.title: Method Hiding vs Overriding (static)
problems.description: Demonstrate the difference between method OVERRIDING (instance methods — polymorphic) and method HIDING (static methods — resolved at compile-time reference type). Show that calling a static method through a parent reference always calls the parent's version, regardless of the actual object type.
problems.hint1: Instance method override: Parent ref = new Child(); ref.instanceMethod() → Child's version (polymorphic).
problems.hint2: Static method hide: Parent ref = new Child(); ref.staticMethod() → Parent's version (reference type wins).
problems.hint3: Return a String[] showing what each call returns: [child-instance, parent-static].
problems.editorial: Instance methods: resolved at runtime based on the ACTUAL object type — polymorphism (virtual dispatch). Static methods: resolved at compile time based on the REFERENCE type — method hiding, not overriding. @Override cannot annotate a static method override. Calling ref.staticMethod() where ref is Parent always calls Parent.staticMethod() even if ref points to a Child. This is why calling static methods through object references is a code smell — use ClassName.staticMethod() instead.

problems.title: Multi-Level Payroll With Override Chain
problems.description: Build a 4-level hierarchy: `Worker → PartTime → Intern → MBAIntern`. Each level's `calculatePay()` calls `super.calculatePay()` and adjusts. Worker: baseRate * hours. PartTime: 80% of Worker rate. Intern: PartTime rate + $100 stipend. MBAIntern: Intern rate + 20% premium. Compute MBAIntern's pay.
problems.hint1: Worker.calculatePay() = baseRate * hours.
problems.hint2: PartTime: `return super.calculatePay() * 0.8;`
problems.hint3: Intern: `return super.calculatePay() + 100;` MBAIntern: `return super.calculatePay() * 1.2;`
problems.constraints: Follow the chain: Worker → PartTime → Intern → MBAIntern.
problems.editorial: Each super.calculatePay() call walks the chain: MBAIntern calls Intern.super, which calls PartTime.super, which calls Worker. The computation unwinds: Worker=800 → PartTime=640 → Intern=740 → MBAIntern=888. The call chain is: MBAIntern.calculatePay() → super = Intern.calculatePay() → super = PartTime.calculatePay() → super = Worker.calculatePay().

problems.title: Composition Over Inheritance
problems.description: Rewrite a deep inheritance chain using composition. Original: `Vehicle → Car → SportsCar → TurboSportsCar` where each level adds one behaviour. Refactored: `Car` composes optional `Engine`, `Spoiler`, `TurboKit` behaviours passed in constructor. Show that composition is more flexible — you can build a TurboCarWithSpoiler without a new class.
problems.hint1: Car has fields: `Engine engine, Spoiler spoiler, TurboKit turbo` — all nullable.
problems.hint2: Car.describe() checks each component: `if (engine != null) sb.append(engine.describe());`
problems.hint3: TurboSportsCar = `new Car(new Engine(), new Spoiler(), new TurboKit())` — no new class needed.
problems.editorial: Inheritance explosion: N independent features need 2^N subclass combinations. Composition: each feature is a separate object combined at construction time. FlexCar with any N components needs only 1 class. Prefer composition when: behaviour varies independently, you want runtime flexibility, or the IS-A relationship is forced. Use inheritance for genuine IS-A with shared implementation. Spring: ApplicationContext composes ResourceLoader, BeanFactory, MessageSource — not extends all three.
